Farn PoLrrrcAL PnacrrcEs CovrrrarssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (9t6) 322-0886
January 24,2003
Jennifer McGrath, City Attorney
City of Huntington Beach
P.O. Box 190
Huntington Beach, CA 92648
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our FiIe No. I-02-356
Dear Ms. McGrath:
This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Planning Commissioner
Keith Bohr under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
'.Act").I Since there is no decision pending-before Commissioner Bohr, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.2
QUESTTONS
1. May Mr. Bohr vote on any matter that is located within 500 feet of his
principal residence?
2. May Mr. Bohr vote on any matter that is located in close proximity to where
his business is located?
, 3. Is Mr. Bohr prohibited from participating in decisions that might affect the
source of a campaign contribution?
rGovernment Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
2lnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice (Government Code $ 83114;2 Cal. Code of Regs. $ 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
t:


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page2
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. Mr. Bohr may not participate in any decision affecting property located
within 500 feet ofhis residence.
2. Since Mr. Bohr does not have a decision now pending before him that might
affect his business, we can only provide a general analysis as discussed below.
3. Mr. Bohr may not participate in any proceeding if a parly or participantin that
proceeding has been a source of a campaign contribution of more than $250.
FACTS
Keith Bohr was recently appointed to the City of Huntington Beach Planning
Commission. Prior to his appointment, he ran for city council, but was defeated in the
election. Now that he is a member of the planning commission, he is questioning
whether his economic interests, or the contributors to his city council campaign ,frdy
pose a conflict of interest for him.
Specifically, Mr. Bohr's residence is located within 500 feet of a redevelopment
project known as the Huntington Beach Project, Main-Pier sub area.3 His residence
consists of a condominium complex that shares corrlmon facilities and grounds.
In his private capacity, Mr. Bohr operates areal estate consulting business. His
business is located in the City of Huntington Beach and he presently has an arangement
with a tenant in a building to occupy space at market rent to conduct his business. The
tenant has a lease agreement with the landlord of the building. The lease between the
tenant and the property owner expired on Decernber 31, 2002, and it is anticipated that
the lease will be renewed. In addition to his business, Mr. Bohr serves as the property
manager for the building in which his business is occupied. Mr. Bohr's letter did not
indicate the amount of income that he receives in this capacity.
Finally, Mr. Bohr ran for city council in November 2002. During the election, he
received campaign contributions from local residents and business owners. He is
concerned that he may be precluded from participating in decisions affecting a source of
a cartp aign contributi on.
ANALYSIS
No public official shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use
his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which he knows or has
3The location of Mr. Bohr's residence to the redevelopment proj ect arcawas verified in my
telephone conversation with you on January 6,2003.


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page 3
reason to know he has afinancial interest.a (Section 87100.) As a member of the City of
Huntington Beach Planning Commission, Mr. Bohr is a public official. (Section 82045.)
Accordingly,he may not make, participate in making, or use his official position to
influence a govemmental decision that will have amaterial financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on any of his financial interests.
(Section 87103.)s
A public official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have amate/,al financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family,
or on any of the following:
"Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or
indirect investment worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.,,
(Section 87103(a).)
"Any real property in which thepublic official has a direct or
indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more." (section
87103(b).)
"Any source of income, except gifts or loans by a commercial
lending institution made in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regardto official status, aggregatins five
hundred dollars ($soo; or more in value provided orpromised to, received
by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made." (Section S7103(c).)
"Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor of, a gift or
gifts aggregating $340 or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made." (Section 37103(e).)6
oPlease note that as a public official whose position is designated in section BZ2OO,Mr. Bohr has
an additional requirement to publicly identify any conflict of interest. He must recuse himself and leave the
room acc-ording to new section 87105. Please contact our advice line at 1-866-ASK-FPPC.
'A public official makes a governmental decision when he or she votes on a matter, obligates the
age/rcy to a course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the agency. (Regulation
18702.1.) An official participates in making a govenrmental decision when he or she negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker. (Regulaliot 18702.2.) An official attempts to use his or her
official position to influence a decision if "the official contacts, or appears before, or othirwise attempts to
influence.any member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency." (Regulation 18702.3.)
"The amount of the value of gifts is adjusted biennially by the Commission. (Section 87103(e).)
As of January 1,2003, the value of a gift that would create a disqualifying financial interest was raised to
$340. (Regulation I 8940.2.)


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page 4
"Any business entity in which the public official is a director,
officer, partner, trusjee, employee, or holds any position of management.,,
(Section 87103(d).)?
Once a public official determines the economic interests that might be affected by a
particular decision, the official must then determine if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeables material financial effect on the identified economic interest.
Since Mr. Bohr does not have a specific decision pending before him at this time,
we cannot give definitive advice concerning each of his economic interests, but can
provide general guidance.
Mr. Bohr's Residence
The term "interest in real property" includes any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by a public official. (Section
82033.)
Mr. Bohr's residence is a condominium complex located within 500 feet of a
redevelopment project known as the Huntington Beach Project, Main-Pier sub area.
Additionally, in his letter to you he indicated that his interest in the condominium
complex included shared common grounds. While not specifically indicated in his leffer,
it appears that he may have an ownership interest in the cofirmon area. If his pro rata
share of the corlmon area is worth at least $2,000, the common areais an additional
interest in real property. (Joehnck Advice Letter,No. 4-87-322.)
Should Mr. Bohrbe presented with a decision affecting the Main-Pier sub area or
any property located within 500 feet of his property or, if his property is the subject of the
decision, his real property interests are directly affected by the decision. (Regulation
18704.2.) As such, the decision is presumed to have a material financial ef[ect on his real
property interests and he may not participate in the decision. (Regulation 18705.2(a).)
If any property that is the subject of a decision is located beyond 500 feet of his
property, his real property interests would be indirectly affected by the decision.
(Regulation 18704.2.) The decision would not have amateial financial effect on his
property, and he could participate. (Regulation 18705.2(bxl).)
7In addition to the economic interests separately listed in section 87103, a public official always
has an economic interest in his or her personal furances, and may have a conflict of interest in any decision
foreseeably resulting in an increase or decrease in the personal expenses, income, assets or liabilities of the
official or his or her immediate family, in the amount of $250 or more over a l2-month period.
8 An effect is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if the effect is "substantially likely.,,
(Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.) Whether the financial consequences of a
govemmental decision are substantially likely at the time the decision is made depends on the specific facts
surrounding the decision. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page 5
Real Estate Consulting Business
Mr. Bohr is asking if decisions affecting projects within close proximity to where
his business is located would create a conflict of interest. Since he is not aparty to a
leasehold agreement with the property owner, he does not have an interest in the real
property where the business is situated. e However, his business is an economic interest
that could be a potentially disqualifying financial interest.
An "'investment'means any financial interest in or security issued by a business
entity, including but not limited to common stock, preferred stock, rights, warrants,
options, debt instruments and any partnership or other ownership interest owned directly,
indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other file, or his or her immediate
family." (Section 82034.)
Mr. Bohr has a sole proprietorship real estate consulting business. If the value of
the business is worth $2,000 or more, his business is an investment under the Act.
Additionally, this business receives income from various clients, including the property
owner of the building where his business resides.
Should his business initiate a proceeding, be the applicant on a particular project,
or be the subject of a decision before the planning commission, his business is diiectly
affected by the decision. (Regulation 18704.1.) This decision would have a material
financial effect on the business and he may not participate in the decision. (Regulation
1870s.1o).)
However, perhaps the business is not the applicant, or the subject of the decision.
When a business entity is indirectly affected by a decision, the effect of the decision
would be considered material if it had a specific economic impact on the business. To
determine if the decision would have a material financial effect on his business, we look
at the financial size of the business entity and the monetary effect the decision will have.
For instance, a decision that would affect a business by $5,000 would have a significant
effect on a relatively small business, but a $5,000 effect on a business listed on the
Fortune 500, for example, would have very little impact on that business.
To determine the indirect material financial effect on Mr. Bohr's business, we
would need to know the financial size of the business. That information was not
provided. Since his business is a sole proprietorship, we will assumethatthe business is
not traded on any stock exchange. With that, if a decision would increase or decrease the
eMr. Bohr indicated that he is paying monthly rent to a tenant in the building. A month to month
agreement is not an interest in real property. Should his rental arrangement change and he enter into a
leasehold agreement with the property owner, his leasehold interest would be an interest in the properfy.
The definition of real property includes leasehold interests. (Section 82033.) The standards for
determining material financial effect on a leasehold interest are contained in Regulation 1B7OS.Z.


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page 6
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year inthe amount of $20,000 or more;
result in the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or result
in an increase or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more, the decision is considered to have amaterial financial effect on Mr.
Bohr's business. He could not participate in the decision. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(a).)
In addition to the economic interest in his business, the clients to his business are
sources of income to him.
The term "income" means any "payment received, including but not limited to
any salary,wage, advance, dividend, interest, rent, proceeds from any sale, gift, including
any glft of food or beverage, loan, forgiveness or payment of indebtedness received by
the filer." (Section 82030.) If Mr. Bohr receives income from any source of $500 or
more, or receives a gift valued at $340 or more,l0 that source is an economic interest that
could be potentially disqualitring.
If any source of income initiates a proceeding, is an applicant, or is the subject of
a decision before the planning commission, the source is directly affected by the decision
and Mr. Bohr may not participate in that decision. (Regulations 18704.1 and 18705.3.)
If that person or entity is not directly affected by the decision, the decision may still have
an indirect effect on that source. The materiality standards that would apply aie
dependent on the assets or interests of the source of income that would be affected by the
decision. Since no decision is pending before Mr. Bohr at this time, we are deferring
detailed analysis of this standard until such time as a specific decision is pending belore
the planning commission. You can refer to Regulation 18705.3 for further guidance in
this area.
Public Generally Exceptions
Even when a public official has a disqualifying economic interest, the public
official might still be able to participate in the decision if the decision will affect the
public official's economic interests in a manner that is indistinguishable from the manner
in which the decision affects the public generally. (Regulation 1g707(a).)
For example, perhaps a decision affects Mr. Bohr's real property interests. If the
decision also affects llYo of all property owners or homeowners, or 5,000 homeowners in
the jurisdiction in substantially the same manner, Mr. Bohr might still be able to
participate in the decision even though he is otherwise disqualified. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1XB).)
Again, since Mr. Bohr does not have any specific decisions pending before him at
this time, we cannot provide a detailed analysis of the'public generally" eiception.
l0 Note that the Actprohibits an elected officer of a local governmental agency from accepting
gifts from any single source in any calendar year valued at $340 or more. (Section AeSb3, Regulation
18940.2.)


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page 7
Should you have a question in the future, Regulation 18707 provides the necessary steps
to conduct a public generally analysis.
Sources of Campaign Contributions
Mr. Bohr ran an unsuccessful campaign for city council in November 2002.
During the campaign he received contributions from business owners and residents. If he
received a camrpaign contribution ofmore than $250 from any source, he may be
precluded from participating in decisions affecting the source of that contribution.
The Act prohibits an officer of an agency from making, participating in the
making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision in a proceedingll if the officer has received a conhibution of more than $250
within the preceding 12 months from a party or participant in the proceeding.12 (Section
8a308(c).)
Additionally, the officer could not receive a contribution of more than $250 from
aparty or participant while the matter was pending before the agency, or for three months
following the date the final decision is rendered by the agency in the proceeding.
(Section 84308(d).)
This section applies to any elected or appointed member of any agency, or
candidate for elective office in an agency. (Section 8a30S(a)(a).) It does not, however,
apply to local govefirmental agencies whose members are directly elected by the voters,
e.g., the city council. (Section 8a308(a)(3).) It does apply to a member of the planning
commission, or to a member of an elected body sitting in an appointed capacity to
another agency.
Mr. Bohr will need to be cognizant of any proceedings pending before the
planning commission whereby aparty or participant to the proceeding has been a source
of a campaign contribution of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding the decision. He
may not participate in the proceeding and furtheE may not receive contributions of $250
or more from a party or participant for three months following the final decision.
I trust this letter, as well as the two fact sheets I have enclosed, will provide Mr.
Bohr with guidance in determining when his economic interests maypose a conflict of
I lTh" t"rrt "proceeding" means a decision involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use,
including a proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modiff a license, permit or other entitlement for
use. (Regulation 18438.2.)
t2A"parly- to a proceeding means the "person who files an application for, or is the subject of, a
proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use." (Section 8a308(a)(l).) A
"participant" means a person who actively supports or opposes the proceeding and has a financial interest
in the decision. (Section 82038(a)(2).)


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Page 8
interest while he is a member of the planning commission. If there is anything further, or
you wish to discuss this, please feel free to contact me at (866) 275-3772.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
IET/jt
Enclosures

source of a campaign contribution?

participate in the decision even though he is otherwise disqualified. (Regulation
18707.1(b)(1XB).)
Again, since Mr. Bohr does not have any specific decisions pending before him at
this time, we cannot provide a detailed analysis of the'public generally" eiception.
l0 Note that the Actprohibits an elected officer of a local governmental agency from accepting
gifts from any single source in any calendar year valued at $340 or more. (Section AeSb3, Regulation
18940.2.)


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
Should you have a question in the future, Regulation 18707 provides the necessary steps
to conduct a public generally analysis.
Sources of Campaign Contributions
Mr. Bohr ran an unsuccessful campaign for city council in November 2002.
During the campaign he received contributions from business owners and residents. If he
received a camrpaign contribution ofmore than $250 from any source, he may be
precluded from participating in decisions affecting the source of that contribution.
The Act prohibits an officer of an agency from making, participating in the
making, or in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision in a proceedingll if the officer has received a conhibution of more than $250
within the preceding 12 months from a party or participant in the proceeding.12 (Section
8a308(c).)
Additionally, the officer could not receive a contribution of more than $250 from
aparty or participant while the matter was pending before the agency, or for three months
following the date the final decision is rendered by the agency in the proceeding.
(Section 84308(d).)
This section applies to any elected or appointed member of any agency, or
candidate for elective office in an agency. (Section 8a30S(a)(a).) It does not, however,
apply to local govefirmental agencies whose members are directly elected by the voters,
e.g., the city council. (Section 8a308(a)(3).) It does apply to a member of the planning
commission, or to a member of an elected body sitting in an appointed capacity to
another agency.
Mr. Bohr will need to be cognizant of any proceedings pending before the
planning commission whereby aparty or participant to the proceeding has been a source
of a campaign contribution of $250 or more in the 12 months preceding the decision. He
may not participate in the proceeding and furtheE may not receive contributions of $250
or more from a party or participant for three months following the final decision.
I trust this letter, as well as the two fact sheets I have enclosed, will provide Mr.
Bohr with guidance in determining when his economic interests maypose a conflict of
I lTh" t"rrt "proceeding" means a decision involving a license, permit or other entitlement for use,
including a proceeding to grant, deny, revoke, restrict, or modiff a license, permit or other entitlement for
use. (Regulation 18438.2.)
t2A"parly- to a proceeding means the "person who files an application for, or is the subject of, a
proceeding involving a license, permit, or other entitlement for use." (Section 8a308(a)(l).) A
"participant" means a person who actively supports or opposes the proceeding and has a financial interest
in the decision. (Section 82038(a)(2).)


Advice Letter No. I-02-356
interest while he is a member of the planning commission. If there is anything further, or
you wish to discuss this, please feel free to contact me at (866) 275-3772.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
IET/jt

No. Mr. Bohr may not participate in any decision affecting property located within 500 feet ofhis residence. 2. Since Mr. Bohr does not have a decision now pending before him that might affect his business, we can only provide a general analysis as discussed below. 3. Mr. Bohr may not participate in any proceeding if a parly or participantin that proceeding has been a source of a campaign contribution of more than $250. FACTS Keith Bohr was recently appointed to the City of Huntington Beach Planning Commission. Prior to his appointment, he ran for city council, but was defeated in the election. Now that he is a member of the planning commission, he is questioning whether his economic interests, or the contributors to his city council campaign ,frdy pose a conflict of interest for him. Specifically, Mr. Bohr's residence is located within 500 feet of a redevelopment project known as the Huntington Beach Project, Main-Pier sub area.3 His residence consists of a condominium complex that shares corrlmon facilities and grounds. In his private capacity, Mr. Bohr operates areal estate consulting business. His business is located in the City of Huntington Beach and he presently has an arangement with a tenant in a building to occupy space at market rent to conduct his business. The tenant has a lease agreement with the landlord of the building. The lease between the tenant and the property owner expired on Decernber 31, 2002, and it is anticipated that the lease will be renewed. In addition to his business, Mr. Bohr serves as the property manager for the building in which his business is occupied. Mr. Bohr's letter did not indicate the amount of income that he receives in this capacity. Finally, Mr. Bohr ran for city council in
